planting.note: Plant stem cuttings (which root readily) in spring in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; guduchi is a vigorous climbing vine native to tropical and subtropical India that thrives in Gulf Coast heat and humidity and grows as a perennial in zone 9b-12, spreading vigorously over trees and structures.

directions: Plant stem cuttings 6–8 inches long directly in moist, moderately fertile soil in spring; provide trellis or tree support — guduchi is a vigorous climber. Tinospora cordifolia (guduchi or giloy) is one of the three most revered medicinal plants in Ayurvedic medicine (the others being ashwagandha and shatavari) — a tropical Indian climbing vine that has been used continuously in Ayurvedic medical practice for over 3,000 years. Modern research has identified numerous bioactive compounds including tinosporin, berberine, and various alkaloids and glycosides with anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, antipyretic, and hepatoprotective properties. The stem is the primary medicinal part — consumed as a decoction, powder, or extract. The plant is a vigorous, twining vine with broad, heart-shaped, bright-green leaves resembling a large morning glory vine in appearance. In India, a respected folk tradition holds that guduchi plants growing on neem trees (Azadirachta indica) — absorbing constituents from the host — have enhanced medicinal potency. The plant naturalizes readily in tropical and humid subtropical climates; it spreads vigorously and may require management to prevent overgrowth in frost-free zones.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or allow to self-seed in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; chanca piedra is a Pantropical annual that volunteers readily in zone 9 gardens and is used throughout South America, Asia, and Africa as a traditional remedy for kidney stones and liver conditions.

directions: Direct sow in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; thin to 12 inches; harvest whole aerial plant before seeding for herbal use. Phyllanthus niruri (chanca piedra or stone breaker) is a small, unassuming tropical annual from the spurge family — widely distributed across the humid tropics of South America, Asia, and Africa — that has become one of the most studied herbal remedies for kidney stones, liver health, and hepatitis B. The common name 'chanca piedra' (Spanish: 'stone breaker') and 'shatterstone' reflect the plant's traditional Amazonian use for dissolving kidney and gallstones, a use with some clinical support in published research. The plant contains phyllanthin, hypophyllanthin, and other compounds with documented effects on calcium oxalate crystal formation (the primary component of kidney stones). As a garden plant, it is a neat, small annual with tiny, oval, alternate leaves arranged on graceful, feathery branches resembling a miniature fern — it volunteers readily in warm, moist gardens and self-seeds prolifically once established. Harvest the entire aerial plant when it begins to flower; dry for use in tea or tincture.
sources: University of Maryland Medical Center — Chanca Piedra
Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Phyllanthus

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky to sandy soil in full sun; Lindheimer ironweed is native to dry, rocky limestone hillsides of central Texas and the Edwards Plateau and thrives in hot, drought-prone conditions where many other perennials fail.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, rocky to sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; once established, Lindheimer ironweed is drought-tolerant and virtually maintenance-free. Vernonia lindheimeri (Lindheimer ironweed or woolly ironweed) is a native Texas ironweed distinguished from common ironweed (V. fasciculata) by its dramatically white-woolly leaf undersides and stems — covered in dense, white, cobwebby hairs that give the plant a distinctly silver-woolly appearance, especially striking against the deep violet-purple of the late-summer flower heads. Named for Ferdinand Jacob Lindheimer, the 'Father of Texas Botany,' this species is native to the dry limestone hillsides and cedar glades of the Edwards Plateau and central Texas, where it thrives in the rocky, alkaline, well-drained conditions that defeat most perennials. Like all ironweeds, it produces brilliant violet-purple, thistle-like flower heads in late summer (August–September) that are extraordinarily attractive to monarch butterflies, swallowtails, gulf fritillaries, and native bees. The white woolly stems and leaf undersides create a beautiful silver-and-purple effect unique among Texas natives and make Lindheimer ironweed a particularly ornamental species compared to other ironweeds.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Wildflowers

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy prairie soil in full sun; Barbara buttons is a native prairie perennial of the Texas and Oklahoma coastal prairie that is essentially unknown in horticulture despite its charming flowers and adaptability to lean soils — it is an excellent wildflower for prairie-style gardens in the Gulf South.

directions: Direct sow in fall in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Barbara buttons self-seeds modestly in suitable prairie conditions. Marshallia caespitosa (Barbara buttons or white prairie marshallia) is a small, delightful native prairie perennial found in dry, rocky, and sandy prairies from Texas and Oklahoma east to Tennessee — one of the least-known and most charming small wildflowers of the southern prairies. The plant produces low, tufted rosettes of narrow, spatula-shaped leaves and slender, 8-to-20-inch, leafless flower stems each topped with a single, globular, 1-to-1.5-inch flower head composed entirely of white, tubular disc florets (no ray florets) — resembling a small, white, powder-puff button with elongated, protruding style branches giving a fringe-like texture. The effect is unexpectedly refined and delicate for a prairie plant. Barbara buttons blooms in late spring (April–June) in zone 9 and is a quiet, elegant presence in prairie-style wildflower gardens. The flowers attract native bees and small butterflies. A genuinely underused native perennial deserving wider cultivation in southeastern prairies and dry, sunny garden borders.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Wildflowers

common_name: African Lily (Blue Lily of the Nile, Agapanthus)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; agapanthus is one of the best-performing perennials for Gulf Coast summer gardens — heat-tolerant, drought-tolerant once established, and producing a stunning display of blue or white trumpet flowers in July–September.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; bloom is best after mild crowding — do not divide too frequently. Agapanthus africanus (African blue lily or lily of the Nile) is a classic South African perennial that has become one of the most widely grown ornamental plants in warm, Mediterranean, and subtropical climates worldwide — producing bold clumps of long, strap-shaped, arching, dark-green evergreen leaves topped by tall (18–36 inch) stems bearing large, spherical umbels of 20–50 tubular, blue-violet to white flowers in July–September. The flower heads, 4–8 inches across and resembling a blue fireworks burst, are a stunning feature in summer borders when most other perennials have passed their peak. Agapanthus is remarkably adaptable once established — tolerating heat, drought, poor soils, and coastal salt spray — and practically maintenance-free. Flower color ranges from deep violet-blue through pale lavender to pure white; the blue forms are the most commonly grown. The plant is also outstanding as a container subject, performing reliably when somewhat pot-bound. CAUTION: All parts are mildly toxic if ingested; sap may irritate skin.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Perennials
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Bulbs and Perennials
